query: dengue hemorrhagic fever importance of monitoring platelet count

retrieved_docs.title: Dengue Fever -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Blood transfusion is indicated in cases of severe or suspected bleeding when the patient remains unstable despite adequate fluid resuscitation and hematocrit falls. Platelet transfusion may be necessary if the platelet count drops below 20,000 cells per microliter and there is a high risk of bleeding. Notably, it is essential to avoid administering aspirin, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), and other anticoagulants. No antiviral medications are recommended, and no laboratory tests can reliably predict the progression to severe disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Dengue Fever -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Dengue shock syndrome clinical warnings: Symptoms include rapidly rising hematocrit, intense abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, and narrowed or absent blood pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: Dengue Fever -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The critical phase: During the critical phase, defervescence marks a period when the temperature typically decreases to approximately 37.5 to 38 °C or lower, occurring between days 3 and 7. This phase is associated with heightened capillary permeability and typically lasts for 1 to 2 days. Before the critical phase, there is often a rapid decline in platelet count, accompanied by increased hematocrit levels. Leukopenia may also occur up to 24 hours before the platelet count drops and warning signs emerge. If left untreated, the critical phase can progress to shock, organ dysfunction, disseminated intravascular coagulation, or hemorrhage. The recovery phase: The recovery phase involves the gradual reabsorption of extravascular fluid over 2 to 3 days. During this period, patients often exhibit bradycardia.

retrieved_docs.title: Dengue Fever -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Common laboratory findings include thrombocytopenia, leukopenia, and elevated levels of aspartate aminotransferase. The disease is classified as either dengue or severe dengue. [11] [12] [13] Probable dengue: The patient lives in or has traveled to a Dengue-endemic area. Symptoms include fever and 2 of the following: nausea, vomiting, rash, myalgias, arthralgias, rash, positive tourniquet test, or leukopenia. Warning signs of dengue: Dengue symptoms include abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, clinical fluid accumulation such as ascites or pleural effusion, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, liver enlargement greater than 2 cm, increase in hematocrit, and thrombocytopenia. Severe dengue: Severe dengue is characterized by dengue fever accompanied by severe plasma leakage, hemorrhage, impaired consciousness, myocardial dysfunction, pulmonary dysfunction, and organ dysfunction, including transaminitis greater than 1000 IU/L.

retrieved_docs.title: Ebola Virus Disease -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Complete blood count testing is essential for evaluating hemoglobin, platelet, and leukocyte counts. Bleeding due to coagulopathy can result in severe anemia, resulting in the need for transfusion. Bacterial co-infections are also often present. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombocytopenia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Ask for any potential exposure and symptoms of infections (viral, bacterial, rickettsial). Assess risk factors for HIV infection. Ask about travel to an area endemic for malaria [25] [26] , dengue virus, and Ebola.

retrieved_docs.title: Ebola Virus Disease -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Testing
retrieved_docs.content: A basic coagulation profile, including prothrombin time, active partial thromboplastin time, fibrinogen, and international normalized ratio (INR), should be drawn and monitored to evaluate for signs of coagulopathy. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Dengue Fever -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosing and managing dengue fever involve a multidisciplinary team of healthcare professionals comprising an infectious disease expert, a CDC consultant, an emergency department clinician, and an internist. Treatment primarily focuses on supportive care, including fluid repletion, acetaminophen for fever management, and blood transfusion if hemorrhage occurs. A confirmed diagnosis is established through various methods such as culture, antigen detection, polymerase chain reaction, or serologic testing.

retrieved_docs.title: Chikungunya Fever -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Chikungunya fever evaluation consists mainly of the clinical findings of fever and polyarthralgia in a person who recently returned from an affected region with an 84% sensitivity, 71% positive predictive value (PPV), and 83% negative predictive value (NPV). [33] However, overlap of endemic and invaded CHIKV and Dengue virus (DENV) areas makes evaluation and differentiation of disease difficult. CHIKV is more likely to cause high fever, severe arthralgia, arthritis, rash, and lymphopenia, while DENV is more likely to cause neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, hemorrhage shock, and death. [34] In light of this, WHO recommends treating everyone for DENV until proven otherwise to improve outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombocytopenia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of patients with isolated thrombocytopenia includes obtaining a CBC, peripheral blood smear, HIV, and HCV tests.

retrieved_docs.title: Dengue Fever -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Untreated severe dengue fever may have a mortality rate of 10% to 20%. However, with appropriate supportive care, the mortality rate can be reduced to approximately 1%.

retrieved_docs.title: Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia -- Differential Diagnosis -- Acquired Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia

retrieved_docs.title: Dengue Fever -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients presenting with warning signs of the disease, severe dengue fever, or having risk factors such as age, pregnancy status, diabetes mellitus, or those who are living alone should be evaluated for hospitalization. Individuals displaying warning signs can be started on intravenous (IV) crystalloids, with the fluid rate adjusted based on the patient's response. Patients in shock and not responding to initial crystalloid boluses may require colloids.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Transmitted Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Transfusion of blood products may also transmit yellow fever and dengue fever. Mosquitos transmit both infections. Nearly 400 million people a year become infected with dengue fever annually in the tropics and subtropics of Africa and South America. Affected patients may experience mild illness or progress to severe liver disease with bleeding and jaundice. A vaccine for yellow fever exists. Chikungunya virus transmission may also occur. Chikungunya spreads typically via mosquito bites throughout Africa, Asia, Europe, the Indian and Pacific Oceans, and the Caribbean. Typical symptoms are fever and joint pain. Prompt diagnosis and management are essential if a TTD is suspected, which may involve discontinuing the transfusion, performing relevant diagnostic tests, and providing appropriate supportive care.

retrieved_docs.content: Repeat CBC to confirm that thrombocytopenia is real.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1053
retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is a broad diagnostic category that includes COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY. Within this category, a specific manifestation is Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester, which is further classified as Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage. Recognizing that spotting in an unspecified trimester represents an early‑trimester hemorrhagic event and is considered a pregnancy‑related complication helps clinicians organize maternal conditions that affect pregnancy, delivery, or the puerperium and guides appropriate assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.content: Human factor VIII is used as a therapeutic agent to treat coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders, helping to correct deficient clotting activity and control bleeding. By supplementing the missing or insufficient factor VIII, it restores normal hemostasis in conditions such as hemophilia A and other bleeding disorders, thereby reducing the risk of severe hemorrhage and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.content: Karmustin and Merkaptopurin are both contraindicated in the presence of diseases of white blood cells, indicating that these medications should be avoided in patients who have disorders affecting white blood cell function or count.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2246
retrieved_docs.content: Tromethamin is categorized within the therapeutic class of Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders, indicating that it functions as a volume‑expanding agent used to restore or maintain intravascular fluid volume. This classification reflects its role in clinical situations such as hemorrhage, hypovolemia, or other conditions requiring rapid hemodynamic support, where augmenting plasma volume is essential. Recognizing tromethamin as part of the Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders class helps clinicians select appropriate volume‑expanding therapies and understand its mechanism of modifying blood components to increase circulatory volume.

This overlap highlights the interplay between infectious encephalitic processes and immune system pathology, which is important for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.
